rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MAVIS DENG
rulings.Calendar Matter: REPORT OF STATUS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. The following procedural issues remain unaddressed: 1) Notice of Hearing (Form DE-120) is required for this proceeding and Box 1 must be checked. (Probate Code Section 12201). All notices must include language about remote appearances. (San Francisco Local Rule 14.1.C.6). 2) If an estate is not ready to close within the statutory time set forth in Probate Code Section 12200, the report of status of administration must set forth the status of the estate, including, but not limited to, allegations regarding notice pursuant to Probate Code Section 9202, disclosure of all creditor claims and their dispositions, requests for special notice, and any assignments or powers of attorney that may affect distribution (San Francisco Local Rule 14.35.J.1). 3) The filing of a report of status of administration shall be deemed to include a petition requesting additional time for estate administration. For the Court to make an order, the report and petition must include a prayer specifying the relief sought (San Francisco Local Rule 14.35.J.2).4) No proposed order has been received or lodged with the court (San Francisco Local Rule 14.7.A). Please have the CASE NUMBER and the CASE NAME ready for the clerk. Parties appearing must check in for the hearing using their FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear in person or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. Please review LRSF 14.1. For Zoom appearance: meeting ID is 160 225 4765 and password is 514879. For audio-only telephone appearance via Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF RICHARD LOUIS LEDON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 8/18/26. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Attachment 8 to the second amended petition for letters of administration filed on 1/8/25 did not indicate who the parents of the uncles and aunts were and when those parents (Decedent's grandparents) died. [LRSF 14.18 C.3.] The uncles and aunts presumably represent both the maternal and paternal sides... (2) First Cousin David Kotta post-deceased. How are his two children his successors-at-law? [LRSF 14.35 G.] If they are his successors-in-interest, their 13100 affidavits must be filed (conventionally). (3) The accounting does not appear to comport with Probate Code Section 1060, et seq. Receipts should not include transfers from estate accounts or proceeds from the sale of assets in the estate, etc. [Probate Code Section 1061(a)(2).] (4) The schedules of gains and losses also appear to be incomplete. They should show the date of each sale, the date of death value of each asset, the sale price, and the gain or loss. (5) The summary of account in paragraph 79 of the petition indicates that property on hand totals $2,897,569.94, but paragraph 80 shows $1,547,899.68. (6) All of the cash, in excess of the FDIC insured amount, is held at one bank. [Probate Code Section 9700.] (7) In paragraph 55, Administrator claims not to have taken any action under her IAEA authority. What happened to the Honda? It is not on hand or on the gains or losses schedules. (8) No allowance or rejection of the creditor's claim appears to have been filed. [Probate Code Section 9250(b).] (9) It would be helpful if the calculation of the net distribution amounts were shown. Please provide a courtesy copy of the petition and any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 8/7/26. (It is not necessary to attach to a petition copies of previously filed papers.) Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (Item 4 of an I&A should NOT be completed when a probate referee HAS BEEN appointed; item 4 is ONE sentence.) NB: In order for this Court to track closed matters, orders to terminate and distribute an estate must begin with the following language: "Order for Final Distribution." [LRSF 14.35 I.6.b.] Lack of courtesy copies delays review. [LRSF 2.7 B. and 14.7 B.]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TUNG-PAK ANTHONY FONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 07/22/2026. Issues necessitating continuance: 1) The declaration "correcting" petition substantially changes the relief requested and is an amendment. An amended pleading requires the same notice of hearing as the pleading it amends (CRC 7.53(a)). Form DE-120 is the requirement notice of hearing for this petition. 2) All notices must include language about remote appearances (LRSF 14.1.C.6). 3) The ending balance of cash assets in accounts must be verified. Verification is made by original bank or brokerage statements or original bank letters signed by a bank officer with the authority to sign, showing the vesting of the account, the date, and the amount of the balance. The statements or letters must encompass, not precede, the ending date of the accounting period (LRSF 14.34.D). Please address the above issues and deliver courtesy copies of all further filings to this examiner no later than 07/08/2026 to avoid additional continuance.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE AMENDED AND RESTATED LYDIAH. VARGAS 2008 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Petitioners' request for monetary sanctions in the amount of $8,180, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010-2023.030 is DENIED. Relief or sanctions sought by the Motion on the basis that discovery responses were not initially verified is DENIED as moot. As the Motion states, "March 11, 2026, Respondents finally signed their verification of their discovery responses." (MPA at 9:9-10.) Insofar as the discovery responses at issue asserted an objection on the basis of attorney-client or work-product privilege, Respondents are ORDERED to produce a privilege log as to those specific objections. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.240, if an objection is based on a claim of privilege or work product doctrine, the response shall provide sufficient factual information for other parties to evaluate the merits of that claim, including a privilege log if necessary. Respondents shall produce a privilege log within 30 days from the issuance of this Order. The portion of the Motion brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.300 to compel further responses to written interrogatories is DENIED IN PART AND GRANTED IN PART, as follows: Without waiver of the objections already stated, Respondents are ORDERED to provide further responses to Form Interrogatories, Set One, numbers 2.3, 2.4, 2.13, 12.4, and 12.6, within 30 days from the issuance of this Order. Without waiving the objections already lodged, Respondent Valdez is ORDERED to respond to Special Interrogatories, Set One, numbers 10 and 11, within 30 days from the issuance of this Order. Movant's request that Respondents be compelled to provide further - or initial - responses to Special Interrogatories, Set One, numbers 36-239 (Respondent Vargas) and numbers 36-226 (Respondent Valdez) is DENIED, without prejudice to the information and documents being sought in some other Code-compliant form. As stated in the Memorandum, "This set of interrogatories contains a total of 239 specially prepared interrogatories to Respondent Reynaldo Vargas Jr. and 226 specially prepared interrogatories to Laura Valdez." Attorney Nagakawa stated in the supporting Section 2030.040 declaration attached to the propounded interrogatories, "This number of questions is warranted under Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.040 because of the complexity of this matter, the quantity of the existing and potential issues in this case, and the utter absence of facts in Respondents' 69 one-sentence denials in their Objections to the Petition: "Respondents deny the allegations in this Paragraph.") Facially, the Special Interrogatories number 31 through 237 address, one by one, each of the paragraphs numbers 2-64, 66-69, 71, and 73-79 of the Objections And Verified Answer Of Respondents Lydia H. Vargas, Reynaldo Vargas Jr. and Laura Valdez to Petition of Jay Vargas, filed October 3, 2025 ("Objection"), in response to the Petition for: (1) Accounting; (2) Copies of Estate Planning Instruments; (3) Suspension, Removal, and Replacement of Trustee; (4) Recovery of Trust Property Under Cal. Prob. Code Section 850; (5) Order For Damages And Attorney's Fees Under Welfare And Institutions Code Section 15610.30 and Probate Code Section 859, filed July 1, 2025 ("Petition"). As the Memorandum states, each of those paragraphs in the Objection is a denial of the allegations in the corresponding paragraph in the Petition. The Special Interrogatories seek the supporting facts, identities of all persons with knowledge of the supporting facts, and all documents supporting each of these paragraphs.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE AMENDED AND RESTATED LYDIAH. VARGAS 2008 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] In the Reply, Movant clarifies that 207 of the special interrogatories are 69 "triplets" of questions, and that apart from those, only 19 special interrogatories are propounded to Laura, and only 32 to Reynaldo. Given Movant's failure to make a reasonable and good faith attempt to meet and confer either in person, by telephone, or by videoconference on the issues presented in the Motion before resorting to the court's intervention, and given the nature of the Special Interrogatories at issue as summarized above and in the Reply, the court does not find good cause to order further responses to these almost 400 total special interrogatories at this time, on this Motion. As to the portion of the Motion brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220 to compel further responses to Requests for Admission, without waiver of the objections already lodged, Respondents are ORDERED to produce further responses to Requests for Admission, Set One, numbers 27, 36-51 (Respondent Vargas) and 8, 9, 28, 35-51 (Respondent Valdez),within 30 days of issuance of this Order (see, Code of Civil Proc. secs. 2033.220, 2033.050). As to the portion on the Motion brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310 to compel further responses to Request for Production of Documents, Respondents are ORDERED to identify the documents already produced by the corresponding Request for Production of Documents, Set One, number, within 30 days from the issuance of this Order. The Request for Production of Documents, Set One, at issue, numbers 5- 18, 39, 40, 42, 61-64 were propounded on Respondents Reynaldo Vargas, Jr. and Laura Valdez. RFPs number 5-18 seek "Any and all documents, including monthly bank statements, check copies, communications, etc. relating to [specified bank account] from January 1, 2021 to present;" RFP number 39 seeks "Any and all documents, including monthly bank statements, check copies, etc. relating to credit card account(s) in Lydia Vargas' name from January 1, 2021, to the present;" RFPs number 40 and 42 both seek, "Any and all documents relating to any and all income generated by any asset of the trust from January 1, 2021, to the present.;" RFP numbers 61-63 seek "Any and all documents relating to Lydia Vargas' [specified insurance policy]." RFP number 64 seeks, "all documents relating to any and all communications (excluding those covered by attorney-client privilege) between You and any person relating to any of Lydia Vargas' Transamerica, New York Life, Prudential, and American Income life insurance policies for the period of January 1, 2021, to the present." Financial information is a constitutional "zone of privacy" recognized in case law. "[T]here is a right to privacy in confidential customer information whatever form it takes, whether that form be tax returns, checks, statements, or other account information." (Fortunato v. Sup.Ct. (Ingrassia) (2003) 114 CA4th 475, 481, emphasis in original. See also, e.g., Cobb v. Sup.Ct. (Tleel) (1979) 99 CA3d 543, 550.) However, that protection in the context of discovery is qualified, not absolute. If the party seeking the discovery has shown the confidential information is directly relevant to a cause of action (a higher standard than baseline "relevancy"), the court must balance the claimed right of privacy versus the public interest in civil litigants access to relevant facts. (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Sup.Ct. (Barkett) (1975) 15 C3d 652, 657.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE AMENDED AND RESTATED LYDIAH. VARGAS 2008 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To (1) Form Interrogatories, Set One; (2) Special Interrogatories, Set One 3) Requests For Admission, Set One; And 4) Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One To
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Russell S. Roeca. Before the court is Petitioners Jay Vargas and Reynaldo Vargas III's Motion To Compel Further Responses To (1) Form Interrogatories, Set One; (2) Special Interrogatories, Set One; (3) Requests For Admission, Set One; And (4) Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One, To Respondents Reynaldo Vargas, Jr. and Laura Valdez. Per the Notice of Motion, Movants seek an order compelling further responses to Petitioners' (1) Form Interrogatories, Set One; (2) Special Interrogatories, Set One; (3) Requests for Admission, Set One; and (4) Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, served on Respondents on October 31, 2025, and monetary sanctions in the amount of $8,180, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010-2023.030 [discovery sanctions], 2030.250 [signature on responses to written interrogatories], 2030.300 [motion to compel further responses to written interrogatories], 2031.250 [response to inspection demand signed under oath], 2031.310 [motion to compel further response to inspection demand], 2033.240 [response to RFA signed under oath], and 2033.290 [motion to compel further response to RFA]. The court notes the Motion is functionally four separate motions to compel further responses to four different types of discovery, propounded on two different people, combined into a single pleading. Movant also failed to provide courtesy copies of the moving papers (SFLR 14.10). This presentation is exceedingly cumbersome to the court in reviewing the Motion and its elements to determine which of the over two-hundred discovery requests at issue in the Motion warrant a court order for further responses, and whether the requested sanctions are appropriate. The parties' papers and declarations appear to agree, generally, on the following events: Petitioner served on Respondents the various types of discover now at issue on October 31, 2025. Counsel had been engaging in informal requesting and granting of extensions to respond to the discovery. January 30, 2026: Respondents served unverified discovery responses and objections via email, with a note that verifications would follow. On March 9, 2026, Petitioner's counsel sent Respondents' counsel a 9-page meet and confer letter. Verifications to the responses served January 30, 2026 were served March 11, 2026. On March 23, 2026, Respondent's counsel asked for more time to respond to the March 9 letter, which was granted to March 30, 2026. On March 30, 2026, Respondents' counsel sent Petitioners' counsel an email proposing that they meet and confer telephonically or over video conference that week to discuss the issues raised in the letter. Respondents counsel did not respond. This Motion was filed April 29, 2026. A motion to compel further responses must include a declaration stating facts showing a "reasonable and good faith attempt" either in person, by telephone, or by videoconference, to resolve informally the issues presented by the motion before filing the motion (see, Code of Civil Procedure sections 2016.040, 2033.290(b), 2030.300(b), 2031.310(b)(2), 2033.290). Failing to confer or to attempt to confer as required is a misuse of the discovery process subject to sanctions (Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010, 2023.020). Movant's declaration indicates that counsel only attempted to meet and confer on all of the issues now presented in the Motion via a letter sent March 9, and did not respond to Respondents' counsel's subsequent proposal to meet and confer on the issues therein presented telephonically or over videoconference, rather than by email or letter. The court finds Movants did not comply with the statutory meet and confer requirements for any of the types of discovery now at issue.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE AMENDED AND RESTATED LYDIAH. VARGAS 2008 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE AMENDED AND RESTATED LYDIAH. VARGAS 2008 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] In balancing a person's privacy interest versus a litigant's interest in relevant facts, the court must consider, at least: the purpose of the information sought; the effect disclosure will have on the parties and the trial; the nature of the withholding party's objections; and the "ability of the court to make an alternative order which may grant partial disclosure, disclosure in another form, or disclosure only in the event that the party seeking the information undertakes certain specified burdens which appear just under the circumstances." (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Sup.Ct. (Barkett), supra, 15 Cal.3d at 658; see Pioneer Electronics (USA), Inc. v. Sup.Ct. (Olmstead) (2007) 40 C4th 360, 371.) A propounding party may move for a motion to compel a further response to an inspection demand if the propounding party believes the response is inadequate - i.e., it contains meritless objections, or incomplete or evasive answers. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(a).) The moving party has the initial burden to set forth specific facts showing "good cause" justifying the discovery sought by the inspection demand. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(b)(1); Kirkland v. Sup. Ct. (2002) 95 Cal. App. 4th 92, 98.) That burden is met by a fact-specific showing of relevance, unless there is a legitimate privilege issue or claim of attorney work product in which case the burden requires more than a mere showing of relevance. (TBG Ins. Services Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2002) 96 Cal. App. 4th 443, 444.) While the court acknowledges that the Petition alleges financial elder abuse by Respondents Vargas and Valdez upon Lydia Vargas, and that the allegedly impacted accounts and assets, within the stated time period, are likely relevant facts in this litigation, the court wonders why RFPs 5-18, 40, 42, and 61-63 were not propound on Lydia Vargas herself, who is a party to the Objection to the Petition, rather than on Respondents. There is no clear showing in the Motion that either Respondent has possession or control of the documents sought by the RFPs at issue, or that either of them is authorized to produce Lydia Vargas' financial information in response to discovery propounded on them personally. Although the Reply references Exhibit Y, a document titled Authorization to Release Records and Information and allegedly signed by Lydia Vargas on August 20, 2025, there is no analysis showing how that document puts Respondents in a position to produce Mrs. Vargas' financial records in response to discovery propounded on them personally. The Motion's request for an order compelling further responses to RFPs, Set One, numbers 5-18, 40, 42, and 61-63 from Respondent Reynaldo Vargas and Respondent Valdez is DENIED, without prejudice to the information and documents being sought in some other Code-compliant form. The request for an order compelling further response to RFP number 64, which seeks information and documents related to the Respondents' communications with insurance companies, within the specified time period, is GRANTED. Further response to be served within 30 days from the issuance of this Order.
